sanskrit: कर्मण्येवाधिकारस्ते मा फलेषु कदाचन। मा कर्मफलहेतुर्भूर्मा ते सङ्गोऽस्त्वकर्मणि॥
transliteration: Karmanye vadhikaras te, ma phalesu kadachana, ma karma-phala-hetur bhur, ma te sango 'stv akarmani
translation: You have a right to perform your prescribed duty, but you are not entitled to the fruits of action. Never consider yourself the cause of the results of your activities, and never be attached to not doing your duty.
explanation:
CONTEXT: Arjuna has just learned that Krishna is the source of all knowledge and power. He has been overwhelmed by doubt about fighting his cousins in the Mahabharata war. Krishna now introduces him to the cornerstone philosophy of action without attachment.

THE PHILOSOPHY: This verse encodes the concept of Nishkama Karma—action performed without desire for personal reward or consequence. The key insight: distinguish sharply between duty (dharma) and its fruits (phala). Your responsibility is action itself; results flow according to cosmic law. This isn't passivity—it's the opposite. It's doing your job excellently while remaining psychologically detached from whether you succeed or fail. This breaks the cycle of desire→action→attachment→suffering.

FOR YOUR LIFE: In consulting, you execute strategy flawlessly regardless of whether the client succeeds. In finance, you make the best investment decision available and accept the outcome. In law, you argue your case with full rigor but don't identify with the verdict. The practical benefit: detachment from results removes anxiety and ego. You perform better because you're not desperate. This applies to exams, pitches, negotiations—anything where outcome uncertainty normally causes paralysis.
carryToday: Do your work completely, care about the execution, stop caring about the result.

explanation:
CONTEXT: Continuing Krishna's instruction to Arjuna, this verse condenses one of the Gita's central themes into a single line. Yoga here doesn't mean meditation or physical postures—it means excellence, harmony, and skillful engagement.

THE PHILOSOPHY: Yoga (derived from 'yuj', meaning to join or balance) refers to equilibrium—a balanced state of mind that produces superior performance. When you're attached to results, your judgment clouds. When you're detached, you see clearly and act precisely. This is yoga. The ancient Vedic concept predates modern psychology by 3000+ years: emotional regulation enables expert performance. This is exactly what cognitive psychology now calls 'flow state'—the condition where expertise meets challenge without anxiety or boredom.

FOR YOUR LIFE: A consultant delivering bad news to a client needs yoga—remaining balanced, neither defensive nor overly apologetic. A trader spotting a trade setup needs yoga—executing it calmly despite fear of loss. A founder pitching to VCs needs yoga—communicating conviction without desperation. The skill isn't the knowledge (stock analysis, case frameworks, pitch deck). The skill is maintaining inner balance while deploying that knowledge under pressure.
carryToday: Excellence is not just what you do—it's the mental calm with which you do it.

sanskrit: बुद्धियुक्तो जहातीह उभे सुकृतदुष्कृते। तस्माद्योगाय युज्यस्व योगः कर्मसु कौशलम्॥
transliteration: Buddhi-yukto jahateeha ubhe sukrita-duskrite, tasmad yoga-ya yujyasva yoga karmasu kaushalam
translation: One who is intelligently engaged in yoga, grounded in wisdom, abandons both good and bad deeds in this world. Therefore, strive for yoga—yoga is skill in action.
explanation:
CONTEXT: Krishna is now moving from abstract principle to practical application. Arjuna must understand that this wisdom isn't theoretical—it directly changes how decisions should be made and executed in the real world.

THE PHILOSOPHY: 'Buddhi' = intelligence, wisdom. When your intellect is yoked to the yoga principle (balanced, result-detached action), you transcend the binary of success/failure, praise/blame, good karma/bad karma. You neither celebrate victories nor despair at defeats. You neither pursue only 'good' actions nor avoid 'bad' ones—you execute the right action in context, knowing the universe will handle results. This is actually radical pragmatism: the wisest person acts without being imprisoned by outcome categories.

FOR YOUR LIFE: A lawyer doesn't say 'I only take winning cases' (attachment to good karma) or 'I avoid difficult cases' (fear of bad karma). They take the case duty demands and argue it supremely. An analyst doesn't get euphoric on bull markets or depressed on bear markets—they trade/invest according to the framework. A founder doesn't pivot just because one product failed (bad karma label) nor persist just because one succeeded (good karma label). Wisdom means: action is determined by current context and duty, not by your emotional coding of past outcomes.
carryToday: A wise person acts according to duty, not according to their judgment of success or failure.

sanskrit: श्रेयान्स्वधर्मो विगुणः परधर्मात्स्वनुष्ठितात्। स्वधर्मे निधनं श्रेयः परधर्मो भयावहः॥
transliteration: Shrayan sva-dharmo vigunam para-dharmat sv-anusthitat, sva-dharme nidhanan shreyah para-dharmo bhayavahah
translation: It is far better to discharge one's own prescribed duty, even though it may be faulty, than to attempt the duty of another, no matter how perfectly it may be executed. It is better to die in one's own duty than to live by another's duty.
explanation:
CONTEXT: Arjuna's fundamental doubt is: should he fight? His kshatriya (warrior) dharma says yes. His personal affection and moral squeamishness say no. He's tempted by the 'cleaner' path of renunciation or non-violence (taking on the dharma of a sage or a pacifist). Krishna now addresses this directly.

THE PHILOSOPHY: Dharma is not universal morality. It's context-specific duty based on role, station, and capability. Your dharma depends on who you are, what time it is, and what you're trained to do. Attempting someone else's dharma—no matter how noble—is spiritual incompetence and eventually leads to failure. This isn't relativism (anything goes). It's role-based ethics. A soldier's dharma includes fighting; a sage's includes renunciation. Mixing them destroys both. The Stoics said similar: Epictetus taught that you have a role to play in the cosmic drama; excellence means playing that role supremely.

FOR YOUR LIFE: You're a finance professional. Your dharma includes making investments, analyzing risks, sometimes delivering hard truths. It's not the dharma of a spiritual guru (detachment from wealth), nor a therapist (unconditional acceptance), nor a politician (everyone's friend). Execute your specific dharma excellently rather than imitating someone else's. Specialization beats generalized decency. This applies to career choice, professional identity, and integrity.
carryToday: Your duty, done imperfectly, is better than another's duty done perfectly.

sanskrit: अर्जुन उवाच। सेनयोरुभयोर्मध्ये रथं स्थापय मे अच्युत। यावदेतान्निरीक्षेऽहं योद्धुकामानवस्थितान्। कैर्मया सह योद्धव्यमस्मिन्रणसमुद्यमे॥
transliteration: Arjuna uvaca: Senayor ubhayor madhye ratham sthapaya me achyuta, yavad etan nirikshe'ham yoddhukaman avasthitan, kairmaya saha yoddhavyam asmin rana samudyame
translation: Arjuna said: O infallible Krishna, please position my chariot between the two armies so that I may see those who desire to fight with me, and with whom I must contend in this great battle.
explanation:
CONTEXT: This is the opening of the Bhagavad Gita itself. Arjuna, standing on his chariot between two armies, asks Krishna to place the chariot in the center so he can observe his enemies before battle begins. What seems like a simple military tactic is actually a profound moment—Arjuna is about to face a crisis of conscience that will lead to enlightenment.

THE PHILOSOPHY: The chariot metaphor runs throughout the Upanishads and Gita. The chariot represents the body, the horses are the senses, the charioteer is the intellect, and the passenger (Arjuna) is the true self (atman). Arjuna's request to Krishna—'position me so I can see'—is really a spiritual request for clarity before action. In Vedantic tradition, ignorance isn't stupidity; it's lack of vision. The moment before a major decision, wisdom demands you see the full situation before acting.

FOR YOUR LIFE: Before executing any major decision—taking a job, starting a company, making a large investment—pause and truly see. Look at all stakeholders, all consequences, all alternatives. Arjuna's impulse to rush into battle parallels your impulse to rush into decisions. The Gita teaches: look first. Think. Then act with total commitment. This applies to career pivots, hiring decisions, product launches, and personal relationships.
carryToday: Before you act, position yourself to see the entire landscape clearly.

sanskrit: क्लैब्यं मा स्म गमः पार्थ नैतत्त्वय्युपपद्यते। क्षुद्रं हृदयदौर्बल्यं त्यक्त्वोत्तिष्ठ परन्तप॥
transliteration: Klaibyam ma sma gamah partha naitatt vayy upapadyate, kshudram hridaya-daurbalyam tyaktvotthistha parantapa
translation: O son of Pritha, do not yield to this degrading impotence. It does not befit you. Shake off this petty faintheartedness and rise up, O chastiser of the enemy!
explanation:
CONTEXT: Arjuna has just told Krishna he cannot fight because he'll be killing relatives. He's thrown down his bow and announced he won't fight. This is despair disguised as morality. Krishna's response is not to debate the ethics, but to call out the mental weakness.

THE PHILOSOPHY: Klaibyam = cowardice, weakness, degradation. Krishna identifies Arjuna's paralysis not as high morality but as a failure of nerve. The Gita's hardest teaching: sometimes your anxiety, your attachment, your 'kindness' is actually cowardice. You tell yourself you're being ethical when you're actually being weak. This distinction—between genuine ethics and rationalized weakness—is crucial. The person who quits because 'the system is broken' (ethical posture) might actually be fleeing because success is uncertain (weakness). Krishna demands Arjuna examine his own mind.

FOR YOUR LIFE: When you hesitate to make a hard decision—fire a non-performer, raise prices, negotiate firmly, leave a stagnant relationship—listen to yourself. Are you being ethical or being weak? Often we confuse them. Genuine ethics sometimes demands discomfort. Weakness masquerades as principle. Learn the difference. This is emotional maturity. Krishna is saying: cowardice and compassion can wear the same mask. Only self-honesty reveals which one you're actually wearing.
carryToday: Question your hesitations. Are they wisdom or are they fear wearing wisdom's clothes?
